title: Breakdown of Fluctuational Electrodynamics in the Extreme Near Field
abstract: Fluctuational electrodynamics relies on the assumption that thermal fluctuations in distinct bodies are statistically independent. We show that this approximation breaks down in the extreme near-field regime, where overlapping evanescent surface fields hybridize optical phonons across nanometric vacuum gaps and generate fluctuating-current cross correlations between opposite interfaces. Using a microscopic coupled-oscillator model combined with a Green-tensor formulation of the Poynting vector, we derive the resulting correlation-induced correction to the radiative heat flux. For polar materials supporting surface phonon-polaritons, these correlations become significant when the hybridization energy is comparable to the intrinsic damping rate and can substantially modify conventional fluctuational-electrodynamics predictions at subnanometric separations. Our results establish a microscopic framework for correlated thermal fluctuations in the extreme near-field regime and quantify their impact on radiative heat transfer.
summary: This work shows that standard Fluctuational Electrodynamics fails near surfaces because it ignores correlated thermal fluctuations. By modeling coupled surface phonon modes, the authors derive a correction term to the heat flux due to hybridization resonance. This establishes a microscopic framework quantifying how strong coupling modifies radiative heat transfer in the extreme near-field.
main_problem: The paper addresses the breakdown of Fluctuational Electrodynamics (FE) theory when calculating radiative heat transfer at subnanometric separations.
main_result: The failure stems from neglecting cross-correlations between thermal fluctuations in distinct bodies, which are significant due to phonon hybridization in the extreme near-field regime.
method: A microscopic coupled-oscillator model is used alongside a Green-tensor formulation of the Poynting vector to derive the correlation-induced correction term ($\varphi_{cross}$).
model_or_system: The system involves two closely spaced, polar interfaces (e.g., SiC) separated by a vacuum gap ($d$), supporting surface phonon-polaritons.
key_observables: The total spectral heat flux ($\varphi_{	ext{tot}}$), the correlation-induced correction term ($\varphi_{	ext{cross}}$), and the spectral ratio $R = \varphi_{	ext{tot}}/\varphi_{	ext{FE}}$.
important_parameters: Subnanometric separations ($d$), hybridization energy, and intrinsic damping rates ($\gamma_i$).
main_assumptions: The analysis is most significant when the hybridization energy is comparable to the intrinsic damping rate; conventional FE assumes statistical independence of fluctuations.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the resonance frequencies and coupling coefficients for hybridized modes (Fig 1), and show the total heat flux compared to FE predictions for varying gaps (Fig 3).
paper_structure: The paper establishes a microscopic framework by modeling coupled oscillators, deriving cross-correlations from hybridization, and quantifying the resulting correction to the radiative heat flux.
why_interesting: It provides a concrete example of how non-equilibrium correlations arising from strong coupling (hybridization) can fundamentally alter predictions in thermal transport, which is relevant for open quantum systems interacting with structured environments.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Resonance frequency of hybridized surface phonon-polaritons between two SiC half-spaces separated by a vacuum gap d. In full line Ω+ and in dashed line Ω−(b) In- terfacial coupling coefficient K(k∥, d) between the two surface phonon-polariton oscillators localized the opposite interfaces for different separation distances.

FIG. 2. Cross-correlation spectrum between the fluctuations of the two coupled surface oscillators for two SiC half-spaces of temperature T1 = 310 K and T1 = 300 K separated by a vacuum gap (a) d = 1 nm and (b) d = 10 nm.
FIG. 4. Enhancement factor R = φtot/φFE for two SiC half- spaces at T1 = 310 K and T2 = 300 K, as a function of fre- quency and separation distance.

FIG. 3. Spectral radiative heat flux carried by correlated fluc- tuations (full line) and predicted by conventional FE (dashed line) between two SiC half-spaces separated by a vacuum gap (a) d = 1 nm, (b) d = 10 nm and (c) d = 100 nm.
